customer_name: Kay Rosaire and Clayton Rosaire Mowrey
site_name: Kay Rosaire and Clayton Rosaire Mowrey

citations.narrative:
At the time of inspection, the facility offered an encounter where guests were allowed to participate in a yoga session,
held inside an enclosure with free-roaming lemurs. During the encounter, the lemurs are allowed direct contact with
members of the public including access to the shoulders, upper backs, laps, and close proximity to guests’ faces.
Nonhuman primates and humans are capable of transmitting infectious diseases to one another, including diseases
spread through respiratory droplets, saliva, or close facial contact. Allowing nonhuman primates close proximity or access
to the faces of members of the public does not demonstrate that the animals are being handled in a manner that
safeguards their health and well-being and protects them and the public from potential harm.
During public exhibition, any animal must be handled so there is minimal risk of harm to the animal and to the public, with
sufficient distance and/or barriers between the animal and the general viewing public so as to assure the safety of the
animals and the public. To be corrected from this day, March 2, 2026, forward.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
n

narrative:
2.131(c)(1) 
   
Handling of animals. 
At the time of inspection, the facility offered an encounter where guests were allowed to participate in a yoga session, 
held inside an enclosure with free-roaming lemurs. During the encounter, the lemurs are allowed direct contact with 
members of the public including access to the shoulders, upper backs, laps, and close proximity to guests’ faces. 
 
Nonhuman primates and humans are capable of transmitting infectious diseases to one another, including diseases 
spread through respiratory droplets, saliva, or close facial contact. Allowing nonhuman primates close proximity or access 
to the faces of members of the public does not demonstrate that the animals are being handled in a manner that 
safeguards their health and well-being and protects them and the public from potential harm. 
 
During public exhibition, any animal must be handled so there is minimal risk of harm to the animal and to the public, with 
sufficient distance and/or barriers between the animal and the general viewing public so as to assure the safety of the 
animals and the public. To be corrected from this day, March 2, 2026, forward. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a facility representative.
